subject: AP Physics 1: Algebra-Based
text:
When two charged, massive objects are placed a 
distance r apart, the gravitational force between 
them has magnitude F. When the distance 
between the objects is increased to 2r, the 
magnitude of the gravitational force between them 
becomes F 4 . Did the electrostatic force between 
the objects also decrease to one fourth its initial 
magnitude as a result of the change in position, 
and why?

options.A:
No, because the gravitational constant is 
much smaller than the electrostatic constant.
options.B:
No, because the gravitational force is only 
attractive, and the electrostatic force can 
also be repulsive.
options.C:
Yes, because both forces have the same 
1 r2 dependence.
options.D:
Yes, because the gravitational force always 
equals the electrostatic force at any given 
distance.

text:
Questions 22-24 refer to the following material. 
A student is observing an object of unknown mass 
that is oscillating horizontally at the end of an ideal 
spring. The student measures the object’s period of

the object’s maximum kinetic energy?

options.A:
Energy was transferred from the object to the 
spring, which increased the maximum 
potential energy of the spring.
options.B:
Energy was transferred from the spring to the 
object, which decreased the maximum 
potential energy of the spring.
options.C:
As energy was transferred back and forth 
between the object and the spring, a greater 
average share of the energy became 
potential energy of the spring.
options.D:
The object-spring system lost energy to its 
surroundings.

text:
Questions 25-26 refer to the following material.

A crate is on a horizontal frictionless surface. A force of magnitude $F$ is exerted on the crate at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, as shown in the figure above, causing the crate to slide to the right. The surface exerts a normal force of magnitude $F_N$ on the crate. As the crate slides a distance $d$, it gains an amount of kinetic energy $\Delta K$. While $F$ is kept constant, the angle $\theta$ is now doubled but is still less than $90^\circ$. Assume the crate remains in contact with the surface.

How does the new normal force exerted on the crate compare to $F_N$?

options.A: The new normal force is greater than FN .
options.B: The new normal force is less than FN .
options.C: The new normal force is equal to FN .
options.D:
The new normal force is greater or less than 
FN depending on the value of q .

subject: AP Physics 1: Algebra-Based
text:
Alan and Beverly are on opposite sides of a large 
room when Alan says something to Beverly.

of the following could be different about the 
second sound wave compared to the first that 
allows Beverly to hear it?

options.A:
The second wave travels to Beverly more 
quickly, so less energy is dissipated by the
options.B:
The second wave reflects more off the walls 
of the room.
options.C:
The air molecules disturbed by the second 
wave undergo a greater displacement from 
their equilibrium positions.
options.D:
The air molecules disturbed by the second 
wave are, on average, more closely spaced.

subject: AP Physics 1: Algebra-Based
text:
Two strings differing only in length are attached 
to the same oscillator, as shown in the figure 
above. Both are fixed at the other end and are 
under the same tension. The oscillator creates a 
transverse wave and is adjusted to the lowest 
frequency that creates a standing wave on the 
shorter string. Which of the following explains 
why there will not be a standing wave on the 
longer string?

options.A:
The waves travel at slightly different speeds 
on the two strings.
options.B:
An oscillator frequency that results in a 
standing wave on a string of one length 
cannot result in a standing wave on a string 
with a different length.
options.C:
The amplitude of the wave does not match 
the boundary conditions for strings of 
different length at the same time.
options.D:
The wavelength associated with the given 
frequency does not match the boundary 
conditions set by the length of the longer 
string.

text:
A student wants to determine the speed of 
sound at an elevation of one mile. To do this the 
student performs an experiment to determine the 
resonance frequencies of a tube that is closed at 
one end. The student takes measurements every 
day for a week and gets different results on 
different days. Which of the following 
experiments would help the student determine 
the reason for the different results?

options.A:
Repeating the experiment on several 
10∞C days and several 20∞C days
options.B: Repeating the experiment using a longer tube
options.C:
Repeating the experiment using a wider range 
of frequencies of sound
options.D:
Repeating the original experiment for an 
additional week

For each of these questions, you must select both correct choices to earn credit. No partial credit will be earned if 
only one correct choice is selected. Select the two that are best in each case and then enter both of the appropriate 
letters in the corresponding space on the answer sheet.

text:
Each of the figures above shows a tractor attached

object on the tractor?

The magnitude of the force exerted by the
truck on the tractor is greatest, because the

resulting motion is in the direction of the
tractor’s pull.

tractor exerts an equal force on each object.

subject: AP Physics 1: Algebra-Based
text: A person holds a book at rest a few feet above a table. The person then lowers the book at a slow constant speed and places it on the table. Which of the following accurately describes the change in the total mechanical energy of the Earth-book system?

options.A:
The total mechanical energy is unchanged,
because there is no change in the book’s
kinetic energy as it is lowered to the table.
options.B:
The total mechanical energy is unchanged,
because no work is done on the Earth-book
system while the book is lowered.
options.C:
The total mechanical energy decreases,
because the person does positive work on
the book by exerting a force that opposes
the gravitational force.
options.D:
The total mechanical energy decreases,
because the person does negative work on
the book by exerting a force on the book in
the direction opposite to its displacement.

subject: AP Physics 1: Algebra-Based
text:
Two objects, A and B, move toward one another.
Object A has twice the mass and half the speed of
object B. Which of the following describes the
forces the objects exert on each other when they
collide and provides the best explanation?

options.A:
The force exerted by A on B will be twice
as great as the force exerted by B on A,
because A has twice the mass of B.
options.B:
The force exerted by A on B will be half
as great as the force exerted by B on A,
because A has half the speed of B.
options.C:
The forces exerted by each object on the
other are the same, because the product of
mass and speed is the same for both objects.
options.D:
The forces exerted by each object on the
other are the same, because interacting
objects cannot exert forces of different
magnitude on each other.

subject: AP Physics 1: Algebra-Based
text: A cart of mass $m$ is moving with negligible friction along a track with known speed $v_1$ to the right. It collides with and sticks to a cart of mass $4m$ moving with known speed $v_2$ to the right. Which of the two principles, conservation of momentum and conservation of mechanical energy, must be applied to determine the final speed of the carts, and why?

options.A:
Only conservation of momentum, because the
momentum lost by one cart is gained by the
other and there is only one unknown
quantity.
options.B:
Both conservation of mechanical energy and
conservation of momentum, because both
principles apply in any collision.
options.C:
Both conservation of mechanical energy and
conservation of momentum, because neither
cart changes direction.
options.D:
Either conservation of momentum or
conservation of mechanical energy, because
only one equation is required to solve for
the one unknown variable.
